summary: Ronacher argues that Python packaging needs a single, dominant tool to deliver a consistently great developer experience. He points to Astral’s `uv` as rapidly accumulating the functionality that previously required Rye, and urges Rye users to shift attention and feedback to `uv`. Fragmentation in tooling, he says, damages onboarding and forces developers through incompatible choices. The community should be willing to deprecate or stop recommending older tools once a clear winner emerges. He concludes that `uv` is the best shot right now, and even VC risk is manageable because the codebase is forkable and improves the ecosystem either way.
key_points: A packaging tool must aim to dominate so newcomers see one excellent, default path.
Tooling fragmentation makes Python feel outdated and pushes users into costly migrations.
`uv` now covers much of Rye’s functionality and is poised to become the unified tool.
The community should eventually stop recommending legacy tools to reduce choice overload.
Even with VC funding concerns, `uv` is likely a net ecosystem improvement because it is forkable.
money_quotes: The tool that absolutely everybody uses has to be the best tool: it's the thing any new person to Python gets to see when they start their programming journey.
Unfortunately that's not the case today because there is so much choice, so many tools that are not quite compatible, and by the inconsistency everywhere.
Domination is a goal because it means that most investment will go into one stack.
I can only re-iterate my wish and desire that Rye (and with it a lot of other tools in the space) should cease to exist once the dominating tool has been established.
But today I'm looking forward to the moment when there will be a final release of Rye that is no remaining functionality other than to just largely alias to uv.
Some of us have pointed developers at `pip-tools`, at `poetry` or `PDM`.
I think that this is our best shot in the community at finding ourselves in a much better position than we have ever been.
Even in case Astral shuts down or were to do something incredibly dodgy licensing wise, the community would be better off than before uv existed.
key_insight: Python packaging needs to converge on a single, excellent tool, and `uv` is the clearest candidate worth rallying around right now.
